interactions.summary: Jiang explains eudaimonia through Achilles: the choice between a long life as nobody and a short life as a remembered hero. He then turns Achilles' quarrel with Agamemnon into the Athenian model of competitive backstabbing for heroic distinction.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture voice, paraphrasing Homeric characters.

interactions.summary: He explains ostracism as Athens' way to manage runaway competition, then says exile was worse than death because citizenship, not individual merit, defined whether one mattered in the polis.

Jiang pivots from Athenian democracy to a general theory of social conflict: not rich versus poor, but upper nobility versus lower nobility, or the people who have a lot against the people who have some but want more.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang lecture voice; comprehension checks are rhetorical.

interactions.summary: He contrasts democratic Athens and oligarchic Sparta, then moves to the Persian Empire and the Ionian revolt: Sparta refuses help because it is inward-looking, while Athens helps because eudaimonia turns revolt into adventure, glory, and money.

speaker_notes.note: All focus refs are Jiang's lecture. Achilles, Agamemnon, Spartans, Athenians, and Ionian Greeks are ventriloquized for explanation, not live speakers.

claims.claim: Jiang presents Achilles as the canonical example of eudaimonia: to be alive is to become the best one can be, even if that means dying young as a remembered hero.
claims.temporal_scope: Interpretive use of the Iliad in the 2024-10-15 lecture.

claims.claim: He argues that Athenian eudaimonia made Athens intensely competitive because only one person can become the hero, so competition turns into backstabbing.
Ostracism is presented as Athens' institutional response to citizens becoming too competitive in the pursuit of eudaimonia.
Jiang says exile from a Greek polis could be worse than death because only citizens mattered, citizenship was inherited, and slaves or foreigners had no political rights.

The lecture's social-conflict model rejects a simple haves-versus-have-nots story: revolutions usually come from the lower nobility, petite bourgeoisie, or middle class who have some status but want more.
Jiang defines oligarchy as rule by the few and uses Sparta's oligarchy against Athens' democracy to mark the two cultures as diametrically opposed.
claims.temporal_scope: Definition and comparison in this lecture.

claims.claim: In the Ionian revolt, Jiang contrasts Sparta's inward refusal with Athens' eudaimonic willingness to help, seek glory, and possibly profit.

signature_moments.moment: Achilles' choice makes the Athenian soul: long life as nobody, or short life as a hero everyone remembers.
signature_moments.source_phrase: I could live for a very long time and just be a nobody, or I could be a hero and die young
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It supplies the emotional engine for Athens' later expansion, rivalry, and self-destruction.

signature_moments.moment: Eudaimonia licenses treason: Achilles asks the gods to help Troy so Agamemnon will beg.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The heroic ideal becomes socially corrosive, not merely noble.

signature_moments.moment: History is not the haves against the have-nots, but the have-a-lot against the have-some-but-want-more.
signature_moments.source_phrase: between the have a lot and have somewhat more
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This compact reversal becomes the lecture's explanation for democracy, empire, revolution, and war.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Athenian banishment or exile for ten years, presented as a mechanism for restraining excessive competition.
Jiang's later-history name for the lower nobility or middle class: people with some status who want more.
Rule by the few; Jiang uses it to characterize Sparta against Athens' democracy.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR renders 'polis' as 'Polish' in one place; extraction treats this as polis-system context.
